Snipara tech stack (summary):

- Frontend: Next.js 14 (App Router)  
- Monorepo/tooling: Turborepo with pnpm workspaces  
- Database: PostgreSQL (Neon) with Prisma ORM (+ pgvector for embeddings)  
- Auth: NextAuth.js (GitHub, Google, Email)  
- UI: Tailwind CSS, DaisyUI, Tremor  
- Embeddings / MCP Server: Python 3.10+ FastAPI (MCP server)  
- Billing: Stripe  
- Deployment: Railway (web + MCP server)  
- Runtime / safe code execution: RLM-Runtime (Docker)  

(Also uses prisma-client-js for the web app and prisma-client-py for the Python MCP server.)

Snipara (RLM SaaS) is a "Context Optimization as a Service" platform that delivers the most relevant, token-budgeted documentation context to a client's own LLM. Core value props:

- ~90% context reduction — compress large docs (e.g., 500K tokens) down to ~5K highly relevant tokens  
- Client uses their own LLM — no vendor lock‑in or inference costs passed through  
- High margins — product charges for context optimization (compute) not LLM inference  
- LLM‑agnostic — works with any MCP‑compatible client (Claude, GPT, Gemini, etc.)

Snipara exposes a family of MCP tools grouped by purpose. Key tools (from docs) are:

- Primary
  - rlm_context_query — full-featured semantic search + token budgeting
  - rlm_ask — quick/simple query (~2500 tokens)
  - rlm_search — regex / keyword pattern search

- Power-user
  - rlm_multi_query — run multiple queries in parallel
  - rlm_decompose — break complex queries into sub-queries
  - rlm_plan — generate execution plans for complex tasks

- Team
  - rlm_multi_project_query — search across all team projects
  - rlm_shared_context — load team/shared coding standards and docs
  - rlm_list_templates / rlm_get_template — prompt template listing and retrieval

- Utility
  - rlm_inject / rlm_context / rlm_clear_context — session context management
  - rlm_stats / rlm_sections — browse documentation structure / stats

(There are ~43 MCP tools in total — see docs/MCP_TOOLS_COMPLETE.md for the complete tool reference with parameters, examples and return schemas.)

Snipara has two pricing families:

- Context (Context Optimization) plans:
  - FREE — $0, 100 queries/month
  - PRO — $19/month, 5,000 queries/month
  - TEAM — $49/month, 20,000 queries/month
  - ENTERPRISE — $499/month, Unlimited queries

- Agents (Memory & Swarms) plans:
  - STARTER — $15/month
  - PRO — $39/month
  - TEAM — $79/month (requires Context TEAM or higher)
  - ENTERPRISE — $199/month (requires Context ENTERPRISE)

Note: Agents TEAM/ENTERPRISE are team-level features and require the corresponding Context plan.

Short summary:
Snipara’s three-component architecture is split across three repositories with a single production deploy. Each repo has a distinct role (web app + MCP server, PyPI client, and an enterprise self-hosted mirror) and must be kept in sync for deployments to be safe and reliable.

Components:
- alopez3006/snipara-webapp (monorepo)
  - Contains the Next.js web app (apps/web), the Python MCP server used in production (apps/mcp-server), and the shared Prisma JS schema/client (packages/database).
  - This is the source of truth for production code and the repo that is deployed to Railway (service: snipara-fastapi → api.snipara.com).
- alopez3006/snipara-mcp
  - Standalone Git repo that packages the PyPI client (snipara-mcp).
  - Used to publish the MCP client library to PyPI; not deployed as a service.
- Snipara/snipara-server (enterprise / self-hosted mirror)
  - Enterprise / self-hosted copy of the MCP server code (local clone at ~/Devs/snipara-fastapi).
  - Not the production deploy; kept in sync from the monorepo for enterprise releases and contains the dangerous init-db.sh used in enterprise workflows.

How they interact / operational notes:
- Single production deploy: api.snipara.com is built from apps/mcp-server in the monorepo and deployed to Railway (railway up -d -s "snipara-fastapi" -e "production").
- JS Prisma schema is the source of truth (packages/database/prisma/schema.prisma). After JS schema changes, you must:
  1. pnpm db:push (apply JS schema to Neon)
  2. copy new models/fields/enums into apps/mcp-server/prisma/schema.prisma (change generator to prisma-client-py)
  3. redeploy the MCP server
- Enterprise repo WARNING: The enterprise init-db.sh runs prisma db push --accept-data-loss. If the Python schema is stale, deploying the enterprise repo can DROP columns. Always sync Python schema before deploying enterprise.
- Files that must be synced to the enterprise repo after changes: server.py, db.py, auth.py, rlm_engine.py, mcp_transport.py, usage.py, config.py, models.py, services/*.py, prisma/schema.prisma.
- Deployment details: the production Dockerfile for apps/mcp-server does NOT run prisma db push (migrations via pnpm db:push), and the Docker build pre-downloads embedding models to avoid runtime cold-starts.
- Reliability criticals:
  - Preload embedding model in FastAPI lifespan and run multiple gunicorn workers (4) to avoid zombie replicas.
  - Never add a Railway startCommand override (it can force single-worker mode and break the multi-worker setup).
  - Healthcheck endpoints: /health (liveness) and /ready (readiness = DB + model).

Quick commands / refs:
- Manual production deploy: cd apps/mcp-server && railway up -d -s "snipara-fastapi" -e "production"
- Verify health and MCP after deploy: /health, /ready, and MCP tools list/call via POST to https://api.snipara.com/mcp/snipara

In short: one monorepo drives production (web + MCP server + JS Prisma schema), one repo publishes the PyPI client, and one repo is an enterprise mirror that must be carefully synced to avoid destructive DB pushes.

Short answer
- Snipara treats the query's max_tokens as the hard "context budget". rlm_context_query (and related tools) search, rank, compress and chunk only the most relevant sections so the returned context fits within that token budget — it returns OPTIMIZED CONTEXT (not an LLM reply).

How it works (details)
- Budget is set per query:
  - rlm_context_query(..., max_tokens=NNN) — full-featured, budgeted semantic search/compression.
  - rlm_ask is a quick tool (~2500 tokens, no config).
  - rlm_plan / other tools accept max_tokens too (examples use up to 16,000).
- Default workflow budgets:
  - LITE mode: ~3–5K tokens (typical quick tasks).
  - FULL mode: ~8–15K tokens (complex, multi-file work).
- Shared vs project allocation:
  - When shared team context is included, Snipara splits the budget (example/default shown): 60% project docs + 40% shared context.
  - Within shared collections the budget is further allocated by category: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%.
  - Projects can override via project_shared_context.tokenBudgetPercent or per-link settings.
- Selection & compression pipeline:
  1. Semantic + keyword search to find candidate sections (hybrid mode available).
  2. Rank and score candidates.
  3. Select highest-scoring chunks until the token budget is reached.
  4. Compress / re-chunk content (summaries, section picks) to fit the budget and preserve relevance.
  - DocumentChunk.tokenCount is used for budget tracking.
- Safety / performance caps:
  - To avoid timeouts, semantic search has practical caps (e.g., hard cap on candidates, stop-word filters, fallback to smaller/faster embedding model for large on-the-fly embedding jobs).
- Purpose:
  - The entire goal is token-efficiency: deliver only the ~5K (typical) most relevant tokens from a much larger corpus so the client’s LLM can answer within its token limits.

Practical examples
- Quick query:
  - rlm_ask("how does auth work?") → ~2500-token context returned.
- Chunked/full query:
  - context = rlm_context_query(query="implement authentication", max_tokens=6000)
    - Result: sections + summaries whose combined tokenCount ≤ 6000 (e.g., 3600 project tokens + 2400 shared-context tokens).
- Planning:
  - rlm_plan(query="…", max_tokens=16000) for large planning steps in FULL workflows.

Guidance
- Use rlm_ask for fast predictable queries.
- Use rlm_context_query when you need controlled budgets, shared-context inclusion, or larger/multi-step workflows.
- Choose LITE vs FULL based on complexity so you pick an appropriate token budget up front.

If you want, I can show example rlm_context_query callables with recommended max_tokens for a specific task you have in mind.

Short answer
- Shared context collections are curated groups of reusable documentation (coding standards, best practices, templates, guidelines, reference material) that teams or the platform publish once and automatically inherit into project context queries.
- They let teams enforce mandatory rules and surface recommended patterns across all projects so Snipara returns project results that are already aligned with team standards.

How they work (conceptually)
- A collection contains multiple SharedDocuments (markdown-like content), each classified into one of four categories: MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, or REFERENCE.
- Collections have scope: GLOBAL, TEAM, or USER. Collections linked to a project are automatically included in that project’s context queries.
- When you call rlm_context_query, Snipara merges project documents + any linked shared collections and allocates token budget across categories so team rules are considered alongside project docs.

Key pieces
- SharedContextCollection: metadata (name, scope, public/private, version, usageCount).
- SharedDocument: title, content, category, priority (0–100), optional precomputed embedding/tokenCount.
- SharedMcpConfig / PromptTemplate: per-collection defaults and reusable prompt templates.
- ProjectSharedContext: junction that links a project to a collection with options (priority, enabled categories, tokenBudgetPercent overrides).

Token budgeting & ranking rules
- Collections use the documented priority/token allocation model:
  - MANDATORY: highest priority (approx 40% of shared budget)
  - BEST_PRACTICES: ~30%
  - GUIDELINES: ~20%
  - REFERENCE: ~10%
- By default, Snipara distributes session/query budget between project docs and shared context (example: 60% project + 40% shared).
- Shared docs are ranked using title/keyword matching and relevance; non-MANDATORY docs generally require at least a title keyword match (or they risk being deprioritized). Stop-words are filtered to reduce noise.

How to create and use them (practical)
- Create or list collections:
  - rlm_list_collections(include_public=true)
- Upload a document into a collection:
  - rlm_upload_shared_document(collection_id="team_collection_id", title="TypeScript Coding Standards", content="...", category="BEST_PRACTICES", priority=90)
- Link a collection to a project via the dashboard (Project automatically inherits linked collections)
- Load them in a session:
  - shared = rlm_shared_context(categories=["MANDATORY","BEST_PRACTICES"], max_tokens=4000)
  - rlm_inject(context=f"Team Standards:\n{shared['documents'][0]['content']}", append=False)  # makes them session-level context
- Run a normal project query:
  - context = rlm_context_query(query="implement user registration API endpoint", max_tokens=6000)
  Snipara automatically includes linked shared context and splits the budget accordingly.

Team workflows & automation
- Use shared collections at session start to enforce standards before implementing features.
- Use prompt templates from collections (rlm_list_templates / rlm_get_template) for code reviews, PR checks, etc.
- Discovery runs can suggest patterns to add into collections (DiscoveryRun → DiscoveredPattern → SharedDocument).

Access, plans, and permissions
- Shared collections are a Team/Enterprise feature; team plan required for team-scoped features.
- Collections can be public (platform-wide) or private (team-scoped). Admins control which collections are linked to projects.
- Use collection priority and document priority to influence ranking when multiple collections apply.

Best practices
- Start with MANDATORY rules (security, architecture) — highest impact.
- Keep each shared document focused (one topic per doc).
- Use priority (0–100) to order importance within a category.
- Update collections frequently so team standards stay current.
- Use templates for consistent processes (code-review, PR checklist).

Risks / gotchas
- Non-MANDATORY docs with generic titles can crowd results if mis-prioritized — ensure titles contain meaningful keywords.
- Token budget misconfiguration can underrank project docs; tune project/shared budget split if needed.
- Linking stale or incorrect shared documents can surface outdated rules — keep collections maintained.

If you want, I can:
- List collections available to your team
- Draft a TypeScript coding-standards document to upload
- Show a sample rlm_context_query that demonstrates the project + shared-context token split

Short summary
- Snipara implements the standard OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization (device) flow for clients that can’t open a browser. The server issues a device_code + user_code, the user authorizes (via browser), the client polls until the device_code is authorized, and the server returns access/refresh tokens.
- Important safety notes: device codes have polling interval & expiry, tokens are stored as hashes (only a prefix kept for display), and POST endpoints must use www.snipara.com (not snipara.com) to avoid 301 → POST→GET problems.

Detailed step-by-step flow
1. Client requests a device code
   - Client POSTs to the device-code endpoint (e.g., POST https://www.snipara.com/oauth/device/code) with client identifier and requested scopes.
   - Server creates a DeviceCode record (see schema) and returns at minimum:
     - device_code (long opaque string)
     - user_code (short human code)
     - expires_at
     - interval (polling interval in seconds; default 5)
     - verification/activation URL the user must visit

2. Present user code & verification URL
   - Client shows the user_code and verification URL (or QR) to the user. The user opens the URL in a browser, logs in (if needed), and authorizes the client.

3. User authorizes the device
   - When the user approves, the DeviceCode row is updated:
     - status → AUTHORIZED
     - authorizedAt and authorizedById set
     - optionally projectId or scopes associated
   - autoProvision may be set to true for plugin/auto-registration behaviors.

4. Client polls to exchange the device_code for tokens
   - The client polls the token endpoint at the interval specified by the DeviceCode record (respect the interval to avoid rate issues).
   - Until authorized, polls should get a pending response. After authorization the token endpoint issues tokens.

5. Tokens issued & persisted securely
   - On success the server creates an OAuthToken record:
     - accessTokenHash and (optionally) refreshTokenHash are stored (tokens themselves are not stored in plaintext).
     - tokenPrefix kept for identification (e.g., first 12 chars)
     - accessExpiresAt and refreshExpiresAt recorded
     - clientName / clientId may be recorded (e.g., "Claude Desktop", "Cursor")
     - lastUsedAt updated on use
   - Client receives the actual access token and refresh token to use.

6. Using the token
   - Use Authorization: Bearer <snipara_at_...> for subsequent MCP API calls (device-flow bearer token).
   - The OAuthToken record is indexed and used for validation and lookup.

Failure modes & notes
- 405 / POST conversion bug: use https://www.snipara.com for POST endpoints. Using bare snipara.com returns a 301 to www which can convert POST→GET and break the device flow.
- Expiry: device codes expire — clients must stop polling after expires_at and surface an error to the user.
- Poll interval: honor the interval field (default 5s). Excessive polling may be throttled/denied.
- Status transitions: DeviceCode.status moves through PENDING → AUTHORIZED (or DENIED) → USED / EXPIRED. Clients should handle DENIED/EXPIRED responses gracefully.
- Storage/security: tokens are stored hashed (accessTokenHash / refreshTokenHash). Only tokenPrefix is retained for display/lookup.

Relevant model fields (for implementers)
- DeviceCode: deviceCode, userCode, projectId?, scope (default "mcp:read mcp:write"), clientId?, status (PENDING|AUTHORIZED|EXPIRED|USED|DENIED), authorizedAt, authorizedById, autoProvision (bool), expiresAt, interval (default 5).
- OAuthToken: accessTokenHash, refreshTokenHash, tokenPrefix, userId, projectId, scope, clientId, clientName, accessExpiresAt, refreshExpiresAt, revokedAt, lastUsedAt, createdAt.

Practical checklist for a client implementer
- Call POST https://www.snipara.com/oauth/device/code (use www).
- Display user_code + verification URL to the user.
- Poll token endpoint at DeviceCode.interval seconds.
- On success store/securely use the returned tokens; send Authorization: Bearer <token> on MCP calls.
- Handle DENIED / EXPIRED responses and show clear UX to retry.
- Respect rate limits and the provided poll interval.

If you want, I can draft a minimal example sequence (HTTP requests + expected JSON responses) for a client implementation.

Snipara uses a strict layered (n-tier) architecture that separates responsibilities from HTTP surface down to the database. Key layers and rules:

- Route handlers (thin controllers)
  - Thin (max ~50 lines): auth → orchestrate → respond.
  - Move logic out of routes into services.

- Auth layer
  - Centralized session, API key and permission checks.

- Service layer (business/orchestration)
  - Orchestrates workflows, calls query layer and other services (max ~100 lines).

- Query layer (repository)
  - One file per entity, pure Prisma calls only. All DB access lives here.

- Database client / library
  - JS/TS: @snipara/database (Prisma Client JS).
  - Python MCP: db.py → async Prisma-py client (get_db()).

- Persistence
  - PostgreSQL (Neon) with pgvector for embeddings.

Enforcement rules:
- Lower layers cannot import higher layers (strict dependency direction).
- Route handlers and services kept small; queries isolated to repository files.
- All JS imports use @snipara/database (never import @prisma/client directly).
- MCP server mirrors the same layering pattern (FastAPI services → rlm_engine → db).

In short: a disciplined controller → auth → service → repository → DB layering with clear responsibilities and strong import/size rules.

Short answer
- Use rlm_context_query when you want relevant, budgeted documentation/context for a single question or implementation chunk (the normal, first-line tool).
- Use rlm_decompose when the task is complex and needs to be split into ordered sub‑tasks/sub‑queries (multi-step features, architectural work, multi-file changes) — it produces the chunks you then feed to rlm_context_query.

When to pick which (decision checklist)
- rlm_context_query (PRIMARY)
  - Single question: “How does authentication work?” or “Where is X implemented?”
  - Quick edits, bug fixes, or single-file changes (LITE mode).
  - You need an optimized context payload to send to your LLM for immediate work.
  - Typical token budgets: LITE ~3–5K; per-chunk in FULL ~4–6K.
- rlm_decompose (POWER USER)
  - Multi-step / multi-session work (score 2+ under Workflow Mode Selection).
  - Feature spans 5+ files, requires architectural decisions, or will be done over multiple sessions.
  - You want an execution plan broken into independent chunks with dependencies and suggested budgets.
  - Use after rlm_plan (or instead of ad-hoc planning) to generate sub-queries to run in parallel or sequentially.

How they work together (recommended workflow)
1. If task is simple → run rlm_context_query(query) → implement.
2. If task is complex:
   - rlm_plan(...) to get a high-level execution plan.
   - rlm_decompose(query=<chunk-or-feature>, max_depth=1..2) → returns sub-queries + dependencies + suggested token budgets.
   - For each returned chunk: context = rlm_context_query(chunk_query, max_tokens=suggested) → implement/test with RLM-Runtime or your editor.
3. Use rlm_decompose when you need to coordinate multiple chunks, or to feed into rlm_multi_query for parallel context fetching.

Concrete examples
- Quick lookup: rlm_context_query("authentication") → get optimized context and answer in LITE mode.
- Implementing OAuth (complex): rlm_plan("Implement OAuth spec") → rlm_decompose("OAuth implementation", max_depth=2) → iterate chunks; for each chunk call rlm_context_query(chunk["query"], max_tokens=6000).

Practical tips
- Try rlm_context_query first for any documentation/code question (dogfood rule).
- Use rlm_decompose only when you need structure (subtasks, dependencies, token allocations).
- If you need many parallel sub-queries, combine rlm_decompose + rlm_multi_query.
- If rlm_context_query fails (500/timeout) fall back to local read/grep/glob per NON-NEGOTIABLE RULES.

If you want, tell me the task and I’ll recommend which to run and with what params.

Short answer: use hybrid as your default. Pick keyword when you need exact title/file/identifier matches or code/config patterns. Pick semantic when you want intent-level, conceptual matches across many docs. Use rlm_search (regex) for exact code/text pattern searches.

Details / guidance
- Hybrid (recommended default)
  - What it is: combines keyword filtering + semantic ranking.
  - When to use: most documentation queries (e.g., “how does authentication work?”, “implement OAuth 2.0 integration”, “rate limiting implementation”).
  - Why: balances precision for titles/identifiers with semantic recall for conceptual matches; gives best practical results for feature work.
  - Typical call: rlm_context_query(..., search_mode="hybrid", max_tokens=~3-8K).

- Semantic
  - What it is: embedding-based similarity search (intent / meaning).
  - When to use:
    - High-level or vague queries where wording varies across docs (e.g., “how to secure API endpoints”, “best practices for token rotation”).
    - Cross-project discovery of implementations by intent rather than exact keywords.
  - Tradeoffs:
    - Finds conceptual matches you might miss with keywords.
    - Slower and more compute-heavy on large numbers of chunks — may use fallback model for on-the-fly embedding for huge scans.
  - Use for: architectural questions, spec-to-implementation mapping, discovery runs.

- Keyword
  - What it is: title / token / exact-term weighting and lexical matching.
  - When to use:
    - You know the exact identifier, filename, function name, config key, or error string (e.g., "Zod schema validation", "src/auth.ts", "billingInterval").
    - Quick lookups for exact matches or navigation to a specific file/section.
  - Why: fastest, deterministic, avoids irrelevant conceptual matches.
  - Use for code search, configuration keys, and when shared context/document titles matter.

- Regex / Pattern search (rlm_search)
  - What it is: regex/grep-style search across files/sections.
  - When to use:
    - Searching for code patterns, exact text snippets, log messages, or multi-line patterns that require precise matching.
    - When you already know the file path or pattern.
  - Use rlm_search first for pattern-heavy queries; fall back to rlm_context_query if you need ranked context.

Practical examples
- “How does authentication work?” → hybrid (or semantic if you want conceptual overview)
- “Where do we enforce Zod validation?” → keyword or rlm_search with filename/pattern
- “Find all OAuth implementations across projects” → rlm_multi_project_query with hybrid/semantic
- “Show the code that references 'billingInterval'” → rlm_search (regex) or keyword if exact term

Performance & reliability tips
- Hybrid is a safe default; semantic-only can be slower on very large projects (server uses dual-model/fallback for very large on-the-fly embedding batches).
- If Snipara MCP errors/timeouts occur, call it first per policy, then fall back to local Read/Grep/Glob.
- For quick, predictable small queries use rlm_ask (bounded tokens) instead of a full rlm_context_query.

If you tell me an example query you plan to run, I can recommend the exact search_mode and suggested call parameters (tool + max_tokens).

Short answer
- When Snipara returns context for a project query, the total token budget is split between project documents and shared-context collections by default: 60% → project docs, 40% → shared context.
- The shared-context portion is then allocated across the four document categories using the fixed breakdown: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%.
- Within each category, documents are ranked by collection/document priority and relevance; higher-priority documents consume the available tokens first.

Details + examples
- Default split: rlm_context_query includes shared context automatically and uses a 60% (project) / 40% (shared) split of the max_tokens you request. Example: max_tokens=4000 → 2400 tokens for project docs, 1600 tokens for shared collections.
- Category allocation inside shared context: the shared 40% is split by category:
  - MANDATORY = 40% of shared budget
  - BEST_PRACTICES = 30% of shared budget
  - GUIDELINES = 20% of shared budget
  - REFERENCE = 10% of shared budget
  Example continuing above (shared = 1600):
  - MANDATORY = 640 tokens
  - BEST_PRACTICES = 480 tokens
  - GUIDELINES = 320 tokens
  - REFERENCE = 160 tokens
- Ranking and priority: within each category Snipara orders documents by relevance + document priority (the per-document priority 0–100). The allocator fills tokens in that order until the category budget is exhausted.
- Overrides and knobs:
  - You can inspect the exact allocation returned by rlm_shared_context (it returns budget_allocation and documents).
  - Projects can override collection allocation via ProjectSharedContext.tokenBudgetPercent (per the ProjectSharedContext model).
- Safety/quality filters: Snipara applies title-only matching / stop-word filtering / stemming fallbacks and minimum title-keyword matches for non-MANDATORY shared docs to avoid injecting irrelevant shared content into the budget.

How to use it practically
- If you need to verify or tune what shared content will be included, call rlm_shared_context(categories=[...], max_tokens=...) first to see the budget_allocation and returned documents, or set ProjectSharedContext.tokenBudgetPercent to change how much of the global budget a collection receives.

The docs do not spell out a full phrase for the acronym. "RLM" is used as the product name (RLM SaaS) — the Context Optimization as a Service platform (i.e., RLM SaaS = the context‑optimization service).
The MCP server requires Python 3.10 or newer (Python 3.10+).

Short answer
- Query usage is metered per Context plan (FREE=100/mo, PRO=5,000/mo, TEAM=20,000/mo, ENTERPRISE=unlimited).
- When you exceed your plan's monthly query quota, further MCP context queries are blocked (the MCP will reject calls for query tools) until you either upgrade your plan or the monthly quota resets.

Details / actions
- All MCP calls are recorded in the queries table (used for billing/limits). Use the rlm_stats / billing UI to see current usage.
  - Example: call the MCP tools/call rlm_stats to inspect usage.
- Options after hitting the limit:
  1. Wait until the next billing period when the quota resets.
  2. Upgrade your Context plan (PRO → TEAM → ENTERPRISE) to get a larger quota or unlimited queries.
  3. Contact support/sales for enterprise options or temporary increases.
- Note: Agents billing (memories/swarms) is separate and has its own limits/grace-period fields on the AgentsSubscription record.

If you want, I can show the exact MCP command to check your current query usage (rlm_stats) and the dashboard link to upgrade.

If rlm_context_query finds no relevant project sections it will return no (or only shared-context) sections — i.e., an empty/insufficient context result. In that case follow the documented fallbacks and next steps:

- Re-check for an API error first (500/timeout). If the MCP call errored, fall back to local Read/Grep/Glob immediately (docs: “IF ERROR … Fall back to local Read/Grep/Glob tools”).
- If the call succeeded but returned no useful sections:
  - Rephrase or broaden the query (remove stop words, add keywords).
  - Try a different Snipara tool: rlm_ask for a quick query, rlm_search for regex/filename searches, or rlm_multi_project_query to search across projects.
  - Include/shared context: call rlm_shared_context() — shared docs are automatically included and may contain the needed rules/standards.
  - Increase max_tokens or change search_mode (keyword/semantic/hybrid) for rlm_context_query if you suspect strict budgeting filtered results.
- If Snipara still yields nothing and you know the file path or need to edit code, use local tools (Read/Grep/Glob) per the “When to Use Local Tools Instead” guidance.

In short: an empty result is expected when nothing matches; retry with different query parameters or tools, check shared context, and fall back to local file search if needed.

Snipara’s core value proposition is Context Optimization as a Service: it dramatically compresses large documentation into a very small set of highly relevant tokens (≈90% reduction — e.g., 500K → ~5K tokens) so clients can feed only the essential context to their own LLM. This preserves LLM-agnosticism (no vendor lock‑in), eliminates LLM inference costs for Snipara, and delivers high efficiency and margins for clients.

Snipara is built as three cooperating components — client, backend, and web — each with a clear responsibility and deployment surface:

1. Client (Snipara MCP)
- Package: snipara-mcp (pip / uvx)
- Role: MCP client used by integrations and tools (CLI, editor plugins, other apps). Sends MCP calls over HTTP or stdio (see .mcp.json example).
- Purpose: Exposes the primary tools (rlm_context_query, rlm_ask, rlm_search, etc.) so consumers can query documentation, run semantic search, and request context optimization.

2. Backend API (Snipara FastAPI)
- Repo / Deploy: snipara-fastapi → deployed on Railway (api.snipara.com)
- Role: The FastAPI MCP server that implements authentication, document storage, and the context-optimization engine.
- Key modules: src/server.py (entry), src/auth.py (API key / OAuth), src/rlm_engine.py (context optimization), src/mcp_transport.py (MCP transport layer), DB/schema (Prisma), plus services and usage tracking.
- Purpose: Receives MCP requests from clients, runs the optimization/engine, enforces auth and rate/usage rules, and returns tool results.

3. Web App / UI
- Repo / Deploy: Monorepo → Vercel recommended (apps/web on Vercel)
- Role: User-facing interface for account management, uploading/searching docs, and invoking MCP workflows through the backend.
- Purpose: Provides a convenient front-end and a bridge for users to configure environment variables, view logs, and manage the product.

How they interact / operational notes
- Clients call the FastAPI backend over HTTP (or stdio for local agents); backend performs optimization and returns results that clients consume.
- The project uses a dual-repo deployment model for MCP server code: changes in the monorepo (apps/mcp-server/src/) must be synced to the separate snipara-fastapi repo and deployed to Railway.
- Railway deploys are recommended to be manual via the CLI (railway up) because Git auto-deploy is flaky; Vercel hosts the web front-end.
- Security/auth is handled by the backend (API keys / OAuth) and enforced in transport/server code.

This separation keeps the client lightweight and portable, the backend focused on engine + security, and the web app focused on user experience and administration.

Short answer
- Snipara measures tokens precisely with tiktoken (cl100k_base) and uses that exact token count to decide what content to include in an optimized context payload. It greedily selects the highest‑relevance sections until the token budget is used, and if a section only partially fits it smart‑truncates at a sentence boundary and marks it truncated.

What happens end-to-end
1. Token counting
   - All candidate sections are counted using tiktoken's cl100k_base encoder (the same encoding used by GPT‑4/Claude).
   - Counts are tracked per section and aggregated.

2. Search & ranking
   - Query is executed in keyword, semantic, or hybrid mode.
   - Candidate sections are ranked by relevance score.

3. Greedy selection inside the token budget
   - The engine walks ranked candidates from highest relevance down.
   - If an entire section fits without exceeding max_tokens, it is included.
   - If a section would overflow the budget but there is remaining budget, the engine calls smart_truncate() to fit the remainder.
   - Selection stops when the budget is exhausted (or no more candidates).

4. smart_truncate()
   - Truncates text to the remaining token allowance.
   - Chooses nearest sentence boundary (., !, ?) so it doesn't cut mid‑sentence.
   - Appends "..." and marks that section as truncated.

5. Response metadata
   - Each section includes title, content, file path, line range, relevance_score, token_count, and truncated flag when applicable.
   - The response also returns total_tokens, truncated (overall), session_context (if available), and suggestions.

Budget allocation strategies (higher-level)
- Per‑query token budget is honored (e.g., max_tokens 4000).
- There are configurable allocation strategies for multi‑document/shared content:
  - Defaults like CATEGORY_BUDGETS (MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%).
  - Project docs are prioritized (project allocation defaults).
  - Token budgets can be split across categories or per sub‑query (allocateTokenBudget logic).
- Hard limits exist (e.g., max tokens per collection) and out‑of‑budget docs are excluded.

Session & system handling
- Session context can be injected and included (up to a configured fraction of the budget, e.g., ~20%).
- System instructions are auto‑injected where required.

Other features
- Multi‑query mode distributes budgets across queries and calls the same context engine per subquery.
- Decompose tool can break a complex query into subqueries and estimate token needs (not returning content).
- Token‑efficiency metrics (compression_ratio, token_reduction_pct, cost_savings_pct) are available for analysis.

Implementation notes (why counts are accurate)
- Uses tiktoken to match real LLM tokenization (cl100k_base).
- Smart truncation ensures the truncated context is semantically sensible.
- API/tool name: rlm_context_query returns a structured JSON with sections + metadata.

Example response (concept)
- sections: [{title, content, file, lines, relevance_score, token_count, truncated?}, ...]
- total_tokens: <sum>
- truncated: true|false
- session_context: "..."
- suggestions: ["..."]

- Explain how to tune CATEGORY_BUDGETS, session fraction, or the greedy policy for your use case.

Short answer
- Shared context collections are reusable sets of team-wide documentation (coding standards, best practices, templates, architecture rules, etc.) that teams upload once and automatically inject into all linked projects so the AI follows your team’s conventions.

How they work (concept)
- A collection contains one or more documents categorized by importance: MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, and REFERENCE.
- When a project links a collection, those documents are merged into the project’s query context and become available to the model for all subsequent queries.
- The system merges and budgets shared context by category (token budget by default: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%) and respects per-document priority (0–100) inside each category.

Typical workflow (steps)
1. Team admin creates a Shared Context Collection (Team plan required).
2. Upload documents (rlm_upload_shared_document) and set category + priority for each doc.
3. Link the collection to one or more projects from the dashboard (Project → Shared Contexts).
4. On queries, call rlm_shared_context or rlm_context_query — Snipara automatically merges linked shared collections with project docs and returns categorized context.
5. Developers implement features or run model completions; the session or query can inject that shared context so the model MUST or SHOULD follow the team rules.
6. Optionally use rlm_list_templates / rlm_get_template for shared prompt templates (code-review, security-review, etc.).
7. Use rlm_multi_project_query to search across all team projects for examples/implementations.

API / tooling you’ll see
- rlm_list_collections: list available/shared/public collections
- rlm_upload_shared_document: add documents to a collection
- rlm_shared_context: fetch merged shared context for use in a session/query
- rlm_context_query: query project docs (automatically includes linked shared context)
- rlm_inject: inject shared context into a session so it applies to future queries
- rlm_list_templates / rlm_get_template: list/get shared prompt templates
- rlm_multi_project_query: search across team projects

Key behaviors & best practices
- MANDATORY documents are treated as non‑negotiable (always included). Use them for security and architectural rules.
- Use BEST_PRACTICES and GUIDELINES for patterns and suggestions; set per-document priority to influence importance.
- Project-specific docs always take precedence over shared collections when conflicts occur.
- Keep documents focused (one topic per doc), use priority scores, and update collections as standards evolve.
- Team features (shared collections, multi-project queries, templates) require the TEAM plan (or Enterprise).

UI features
- Collections can be linked/reordered in the Project Shared Context Manager; reorder affects priority.
- Linking may surface conflict warnings; admins can acknowledge and proceed.
- Collections have scope (GLOBAL/TEAM/USER), visibility flags (isPublic/isOfficial), and usage metrics.

Use cases
- Enforce Zod validation, security rules, database access patterns.
- Standardize code-review checklists and prompt templates.
- Find canonical implementations across projects (e.g., “How did we do OAuth?”) and ensure new code follows team standards automatically.

Short answer
- Snipara implements the standard OAuth 2.0 Device Flow (RFC 8628). The CLI starts the flow, tells you to visit a URL and enter a short code in your browser, you authenticate there, and the CLI polls the server until an access token is issued.

How it works (step‑by‑step)
1. CLI initiates the device flow and displays:
   - A URL to open in a browser
   - A user code to enter at that URL
2. The user opens the URL, enters the code, and signs in/consents in the browser.
3. Meanwhile the CLI polls the token endpoint until the user completes authentication or the flow times out. Polling request example:
   {
     "grant_type": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code",
     "device_code": "abc123...",
     "client_id": "snipara_cli"
   }
4. Possible polling responses:
   - Pending: { "error": "authorization_pending" } — keep polling.
   - Success: returns an access token and refresh token, e.g.:
     {
       "access_token": "snipara_at_...",
       "token_type": "Bearer",
       "expires_in": 2592000,
       "refresh_token": "snipara_rt_..."
     }
     (expires_in in the example is 2,592,000 seconds ≈ 30 days)
5. The client stores the token (used from ~/.snipara/tokens.json by default) and sends it on requests as:
   Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...

Server-side notes
- Device flow endpoints are under /api/auth/device/ (token polling shown at POST /api/oauth/device/token).
- Access tokens use the snipara_at_ prefix and are validated on the server via DB lookup and expiry checks; scopes are enforced.
- If a token is invalid/expired, the server returns HTTP 401 with instructions to re-authenticate.

Practical tips / alternatives
- The client prefers stored OAuth tokens in ~/.snipara/tokens.json, falling back to an API key if present.
- To force API key authentication instead of OAuth:
  - Set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true in the MCP client env, or
  - Remove/backup ~/.snipara/tokens.json
- If re-authentication is needed the user sees: "Invalid or expired OAuth token. Re-authenticate at https://snipara.com/dashboard or run /snipara:quickstart"

If you want, I can show a small example CLI polling loop or explain how to inspect the tokens file.

Snipara uses a strict layered architecture (controller → service → repository → utils) with an explicit import hierarchy:  

- Routes (app/api/) — thin controllers/route handlers  
  ↓ imports  
- Services (lib/services/) — external API adapters, lazy init + retry logic  
  ↓ imports  
- Repositories (lib/db/queries/) — centralized CRUD and permission checks (always use this layer for DB access)  
  ↓ imports  
- Utils & Types — pure utilities and type defs (no dependencies)

Rules: lower layers must not import higher ones; route handlers must authenticate, validate (Zod), delegate to a service/repository, and return a handled response.

Short answer
- Snipara reduces a large codebase (500K tokens) down to a small, relevant context (typical example: ~5K tokens). That’s a 100× compression ratio and ~99% fewer tokens included in the prompt.
- With a 128K context window this means your LLM can easily accept the optimized context (5K) and still have ~123K tokens free for the prompt, few‑shot examples and a long response — no multi‑call chunking required.
- Cost-wise you save roughly the same share of input token cost (≈99% vs. sending the full 500K, and ≈95–97% vs. filling a 128K window). Exact $ savings depend on your model’s per‑token price and request volume.

Why and how Snipara helps (brief)
- Index + relevance ranking: it finds the most relevant sections for the query.  
- Token budgeting: greedily picks the highest‑relevance sections until your max_tokens budget is met.  
- Smart truncation: trims sections at sentence boundaries to exactly fit remaining tokens.  
- Session context: keeps compacted conversation state when useful (within budget).  
- Result: you send only the useful ~5K tokens instead of trying to send the whole repo or many chunks.

Numbers and formulas you can use
- Compression ratio = full_docs / optimized_context = 500,000 / 5,000 = 100×.  
- Token reduction % = 1 − (optimized_context / full_docs) = 1 − (5k / 500k) = 99%.  
- If billed per‑token, approximate input token cost savings % ≈ token reduction % (input) — outputs still cost the same.

Concrete example (hypothetical pricing)
- Assume input price = $0.03 per 1k input tokens.
  - Sending 128K tokens as context (filling window) costs: 128 × $0.03 = $3.84 per request (input only).
  - Sending 5K tokens (Snipara result) costs: 5 × $0.03 = $0.15 per request.
  - Per request input saving ≈ $3.84 − $0.15 = $3.69 (~96% reduction vs. a full 128K send).  
- If you make 10,000 requests/month, monthly input cost:
  - Full 128K: 10,000 × $3.84 = $38,400
  - Snipara 5K: 10,000 × $0.15 = $1,500
  - Monthly saving ≈ $36,900

Notes and caveats
- Exact $ savings depend on:
  - Your model’s pricing (input vs output rates).
  - Whether you were previously sending full docs, filling the window, or doing multiple chunked calls.
  - How many tokens the model returns (output tokens are billed separately).
- Beyond direct token cost, Snipara reduces latency, number of LLM calls, and complexity (no expensive chunking and reassembly), which also lowers operational cost and error surface.

- expected queries per month,
- whether you currently fill the 128K window or make multiple chunked calls.

Short answer
- Use rlm_context_query for single-step documentation Q&A or code lookups (fast, cheap, one-shot semantic retrieval).
- Use rlm_decompose when the problem is complex and needs to be split into sub-questions you want to inspect/modify or run in parallel.

When to pick each (details)

1) rlm_context_query — primary tool (use this FIRST for most doc/code questions)
- Best for: “Where is auth handled?”, “How does authentication work?”, “List API endpoints”, short/clear queries.
- Why: Hybrid semantic search + token budgeting returns optimized context in one call (shared context is included first), lower latency and cost.
- Good properties: Single-shot, includes shared best-practices by default, tuned for documentation Q&A.

2) rlm_decompose — power-user/orchestration tool
- Best for: Large, multi-step tasks that should be broken into sub-problems (design/implementation plans, research across many facets).
- Why: It breaks a complex query into sub-queries you can inspect/adjust before executing. Use when you want manual control over the sub-queries or to run them in parallel.
- Typical follow-up: feed the sub-queries to rlm_multi_query (or rlm_context_query per subquery) to gather context for each part.

Practical decision rule
- If you expect one clear answer or a short set of documents → rlm_context_query.
- If the question naturally decomposes (multiple related features, architectural plan, cross-cutting concerns) or you want to control/inspect sub-questions → rlm_decompose → rlm_multi_query/rlm_context_query for each subquery.

Example
- “How do we add email verification to signup?” — start with rlm_decompose to get: ["signup flow", "email service", "verification token lifecycle", "UX/email templates"], then run rlm_multi_query on those.
- “Where is JWT created and validated?” — use rlm_context_query.

Notes
- rlm_decompose is a power-user tool (more orchestration). Use it when the orchestration benefits outweigh the extra overhead.

Use these search modes based on the kind of question and how precise vs. conceptual you need the results to be.

- Keyword
  - Best for: exact, lexical matches and narrow lookups.
  - Good when you know file names, function names, error codes, or exact phrases.
  - Examples: "where is auth middleware defined", "API endpoint /users", "error code 500 handler"
  - Notes: fast, predictable, and works on Free plan.

- Semantic
  - Best for: conceptual, “how does X work?” or architecture-level questions where wording varies.
  - Good when you need understanding across files, design rationale, or similar-but-not-identical wording.
  - Examples: "How does authentication work?", "shareable context pattern", "project architecture for contexts"
  - Notes: more robust to wording but requires Pro+ (may be downgraded on Free).

- Hybrid (keyword + semantic)
  - Best for: general documentation searches and mixed needs — you want both precise hits and conceptually relevant sections.
  - Good for: best-practices lookup, broad API discovery, or when you’re unsure which mode will work best.
  - Examples: "List all API endpoints and how they authenticate", "best practices for shared contexts"
  - Notes: typically gives the best overall results; requires Pro+ (downgraded to keyword on Free).

Other operational notes
- If you’re unsure, choose hybrid for best recall and relevance.
- On Free plan, semantic/hybrid requests are automatically downgraded to keyword; the response will include search_mode_downgraded: true and a warning.
- For very quick, small queries use rlm_ask (quick keyword-style query) instead of a full context_query.

Authentication options available

1. API key (X-API-Key: rlm_...)
2. OAuth 2.0 device-flow tokens (Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...)
3. Host/endpoint-specific differences (MCP vs Inject API) — which headers each accepts and how they are used

How they differ in security

- API keys
  - Format and storage: keys start with the rlm_ prefix. The server stores SHA‑256 hashes of keys in the DB (hash comparison on lookup).
  - Visibility/logging: only a short prefix/first 8 chars are shown in the UI/logs to reduce exposure.
  - Lifetime and revocation: API keys are typically long‑lived until you rotate/revoke them (dashboard). The system checks project keys first, then team keys (dual path).
  - Transport: sent as X-API-Key header for both MCP and Inject APIs (MCP also accepts Authorization bearer).
  - Security characteristics: static secret — if leaked it can be used until revoked; less granular than OAuth unless you create different keys for different scopes/projects. Good for server-to-server or automation where a static secret is needed.

- OAuth device-flow tokens
  - Format and storage: access tokens have prefix snipara_at_; access and refresh tokens are stored as hashes in the DB and tokenPrefix (first 12 chars) is kept for identification.
  - Scopes & granularity: tokens include scopes (e.g., "mcp:read mcp:write") so you can restrict capabilities per token.
  - Lifetime & refresh: access tokens expire (accessExpiresAt) and can be refreshed with refresh tokens (refreshExpiresAt may exist). Shorter‑lived access tokens reduce window of abuse.
  - Revocation and listing: there are API endpoints to list and revoke tokens (GET /api/settings/tokens, DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[id]).
  - Device flow UX: uses RFC 8628 device flow (CLI prints a code and you authenticate in browser) so you don’t have to copy/paste API keys.
  - Security characteristics: more secure for user-auth scenarios — tokens are short‑lived, scope-limited, and revocable. However, client-side caching (e.g., ~/.snipara/tokens.json) can persist credentials; the MCP server may cache OAuth credentials and you may need to restart the client process (or remove tokens.json) to force different behavior.

- Endpoint differences (MCP vs Inject)
  - MCP Server: accepts X-API-Key or Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_ — supports both API key and OAuth token authentication.
  - Inject API: accepts X-API-Key only (API-key-only).
  - Choosing endpoint affects which auth method is usable.

Practical security trade-offs and recommendations
- Use OAuth device-flow tokens for interactive user authentication and when you want scope-limited, short‑lived credentials that are easy to revoke and audit.
- Use API keys for automated server-to-server use or tooling that needs a stable secret; keep keys minimal in scope, rotate them, and store them securely.
- Protect client-side token files (e.g., ~/.snipara/tokens.json). If you need to force API-key authentication, remove or back up that file (rm or mv ~/.snipara/tokens.json) and restart the client.
- Revoke tokens from the dashboard or via DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[id] if compromised.
- Remember: OAuth tokens may be cached by processes — restart the client/server to clear cached credentials after revocation or removal.

If you want, I can recommend which method fits your specific use case (CLI user vs automation) or show commands to test each authentication flow.

Short answer
- The system splits the model token budget between project-specific docs and shared-context documents by category, then picks documents to include until each category’s allocated token sub‑budget is exhausted.
- Allocation is driven by a default allocation map, can be overridden via parameters, and enforces per‑collection hard limits.

How it works (implementation details)
1. Allocation map
   - The concrete default used in code (apps/web/lib/services/token-budget.ts) is:
     - project: 40%
     - MANDATORY: 25%
     - BEST_PRACTICES: 20%
     - GUIDELINES: 10%
     - REFERENCE: 5%
   - You can supply a customAllocation to override these percentages when calling allocateTokenBudget.

2. Category ordering and who is allocated first
   - Project docs are allocated first using the project sub‑budget (project percent × totalBudget).
   - Shared docs are allocated per category using each category’s sub‑budget (category percent × totalBudget). Conceptually MANDATORY has the highest priority.

3. Selecting docs within each bucket
   - For each bucket (project or a shared category) the code:
     - Sorts docs by relevanceScore (if present), then by existing priority.
     - Adds documents until the bucket’s token budget is exhausted.
     - Docs that don’t fit are marked allocated: false with allocationReason "exceeded budget".

4. Hard limits and enforcement
   - MAX_COLLECTIONS_PER_PROJECT, MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION (100,000), MAX_DOCS_PER_COLLECTION, MIN_RELEVANCE_THRESHOLD are enforced.
   - enforceCollectionLimits will truncate a collection’s documents to keep the total tokens ≤ MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION.

5. Runtime parameters and APIs that affect allocation
   - rlm_context_query supports include_shared_context and shared_context_budget_percent (e.g., set shared_context_budget_percent to give a specific percent of the total to shared context).
   - rlm_shared_context supports category filters and max_tokens.
   - allocateTokenBudget accepts customAllocation to change per‑category percentages programmatically.

Example
- totalBudget = 4000 and default allocation map:
  - projectBudget = 0.40 × 4000 = 1600 tokens (allocated first)
  - MANDATORY = 0.25 × 4000 = 1000 tokens
  - BEST_PRACTICES = 800 tokens
  - GUIDELINES = 400 tokens
  - REFERENCE = 200 tokens
- Documents in each bucket are included (highest relevance first) until each bucket’s tokens are used up.

Notes / best practices
- MANDATORY rules should always be included when relevant (highest shared priority).
- Use the rlm_shared_context and rlm_context_query parameters to tune how much of the budget shared context consumes (e.g., increase shared_context_budget_percent when you want stronger enforcement of team standards).
- Keep shared documents focused and prioritize their internal priority field so the allocator picks the most important items first.

Short answer: The Snipara docs you provided don’t include a built-in “real-time collaborative editing” feature. For multi-user editing you should treat the work as a FULL-mode project and add a collaboration layer around Snipara. Below is a concise, practical plan (architecture + implementation checklist) you can follow.

Why FULL mode
- The workflow’s Mode Scoring marks “Multiple sessions needed? (+1)”, so real-time/multi-user editing pushes you into FULL (Plan → Decompose → Per-chunk).
- FULL mode supports per-chunk handling, memory, and safe execution which you’ll need for coordinating concurrent edits.

Recommended architecture (high level)
1. Collaboration layer (client + server)
   - Use a CRDT (recommended: Yjs) or OT (if you have existing infra) for conflict-free concurrent edits.
   - Clients connect via WebSocket or WebRTC (Yjs has connectors for both).
   - Server persists documents (Yjs + a persistence adapter, e.g., LevelDB/Postgres) and forwards updates to other clients.

2. Document model & storage
   - Store canonical source files (git repo or object store). Keep CRDT state separate but persisted so sessions can reconnect.
   - Use .mcp.json and the project index (Phase 0: DOC PREP) as authoritative metadata.

3. Integration with Snipara tools
   - rlm_context_query: provide each user’s editor session with optimized context snippets for the file or selection they’re editing.

If no relevant documents are found, rlm_context_query will return an empty (or effectively empty) context plus metadata and suggestions — not an error. Concretely:

- sections: [] (no sections selected)
- total_tokens: 0
- truncated: false
- session_context: included if there is prior session context
- suggestions: a list of fallback hints (e.g., other docs to check or next actions)
- first_query_tips_included: true only on the session’s first call (you may still get the tool tips)

What you can do next (recommended fallbacks)
- Retry with a broader search_mode (use "hybrid" or "semantic") or increase max_tokens so more candidate sections are considered.
- Include or increase shared context (include_shared_context=true) so shared best-practices sections are returned first.
- Run rlm_multi_query or rlm_decompose to break the question into sub-queries or run parallel queries.
- Use rlm_search or a site-wide search to locate documents by filename or keywords.
- Ask the assistant directly for a general explanation (it can answer from knowledge even without project docs).
- If you expect docs to exist but they don’t show up, the QueryCache may be stale or documents may be missing—check that source documents are present and up-to-date.

You can detect the “no results” case by checking for an empty sections array and total_tokens == 0 in the response, then apply one of the fallback strategies above.

Summary of how Snipara's token budgeting works (from the documentation):

Key ideas and defaults
- Token counting uses tiktoken with the cl100k_base encoder (project requires tiktoken>=0.5.0).
- Context queries use a new tool rlm_context_query with params:
  - query: string
  - max_tokens: int (default 4000) — total token budget for the returned context
  - search_mode: "keyword" | "semantic" | "hybrid"
  - include_metadata: bool
- The context-query response includes: sections (title, content, file, lines, relevance_score, token_count), total_tokens, truncated flag, session_context, and suggestions.

Selection algorithm (how the budget is spent)
1. Search & ranking
   - Candidate sections are found using the requested search_mode (keyword, semantic, or hybrid).
   - Candidates are ranked by relevance score.

2. Token-counting
   - Each candidate’s token count is computed with cl100k_base.

3. Greedy selection within budget
   - Sections are selected in descending relevance order until adding a full section would exceed max_tokens.
   - If a section would partially fit, a smart truncation is applied to use the remaining tokens (see below).
   - The engine stops when the budget would be exceeded (or budget reached).

Smart truncation (smart_truncate)
- When only a partial section can fit, content is truncated to the remaining token allowance.
- Truncation is done at sentence boundaries (. ! ?) (i.e., don’t cut mid-sentence).
- An ellipsis ("...") is appended and the section metadata includes truncated: true.

Other budgeting policies & allocations
- There are category-based allocation patterns in the docs:
  - CATEGORY_BUDGETS example (shared categories): MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%.
  - A DEFAULT_ALLOCATION (web token-budget logic) shows: project 40%, MANDATORY 25%, BEST_PRACTICES 20%, GUIDELINES 10%, REFERENCE 5%.
- The web-side allocation implementation:
  - Allocates project docs first (using the project fraction of the total budget).
  - Then allocates shared docs by category according to the configured fractions.
  - Within each allocation, docs are sorted by relevance and added until the category budget is used.
- Category priorities are defined (e.g., MANDATORY highest priority).

Session & system context
- Session context (previous context) can be included in the response; implementation notes mention it may be limited (session context inclusion up to 20% of the budget).
- System instructions are auto-injected into the process.

Metadata, limits, and reporting
- Returned metadata per section: file path, line ranges, relevance score, token_count, and truncated flag when applicable.
- Hard limits / safeguards exist in code (examples: MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION = 100,000; MIN_RELEVANCE_THRESHOLD = 0.2).
- The rlm_context_query response includes usage/search metadata (e.g., total_tokens used, truncated boolean, suggestions, search time and sections scanned in tooling outputs).

Typical flow for a context query (concise)
1. Run keyword/semantic/hybrid search for query.
2. Rank candidate sections by relevance.
3. Count tokens (tiktoken).
4. Greedily add highest-relevance sections until budget is filled.
5. If only a partial section fits, smart_truncate at sentence boundaries and mark truncated.
6. Return selected sections plus metadata, total_tokens, truncated flag, session_context, and suggestions.

Short answer
- Shared context collections are reusable collections of team-wide documents (coding standards, best practices, guidelines, templates) that teams upload once and automatically make available to all linked projects.
- When a project is linked to a collection, those documents are merged into query context and used to influence answers/implementations so team rules are followed.

How they work (facts from the docs)
- Purpose
  - Let teams enforce consistent coding standards, architecture/security non‑negotiables, and recommended patterns across projects.
- Creation & linking
  - Team admin creates a Shared Context Collection and uploads documents (rlm_upload_shared_document).
  - Collections are linked to projects via the dashboard (Project > Shared Context). Linked projects automatically inherit the collection’s documents.
  - You can list available collections (team + public) with rlm_list_collections(include_public=true).
- Document categories & priorities
  - Documents are categorized and prioritized into four categories (used to allocate token budget and ordering):
    - MANDATORY (highest) — non‑negotiable rules (40% of shared budget)
    - BEST_PRACTICES (high) — recommended patterns (30%)
    - GUIDELINES (medium) — suggestions (20%)
    - REFERENCE (low) — background/examples (10%)
  - Each document also has a priority (0–100) within its category.
- How queries include shared context
  - Use rlm_shared_context to fetch merged shared context (optionally filtered by categories and max_tokens).
  - rlm_context_query automatically includes shared context from linked collections when querying a project. Shared context is loaded first, then local project docs are queried with the remaining token budget.
  - Documentation shows common splits such as ~30–40% of the token budget used for shared context (remaining 60–70% for project docs); within the shared portion the category percentages above are applied.
- Tools & templates
  - Prompt templates and review/checklist templates can live in shared collections; list/get them with rlm_list_templates and rlm_get_template.
  - Multi‑project search across team projects uses rlm_multi_project_query.
- Typical workflow (from docs)
  1. Admin uploads team standards to a collection.
  2. Link the collection to projects.
  3. Developers fetch the shared context (rlm_shared_context) or run rlm_context_query; the team rules are auto‑injected into subsequent work.
  4. Optionally inject the shared content into a session (rlm_inject) so all later completions enforce standards.
- Access / plan requirements
  - Shared Collections and related team features require the Team (or Enterprise) plan — not available on Free/Pro according to the docs.

When to use
- Enforce mandatory security/architecture rules
- Ensure consistent code style, testing, or design patterns across projects
- Provide team templates (code review checklists, feature specs) for consistency

References (from provided docs)
- Examples and API calls: rlm_upload_shared_document, rlm_shared_context, rlm_context_query, rlm_list_collections, rlm_list_templates, rlm_get_template, rlm_multi_project_query.
- Category token allocations: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%.
- MCP Query Order: shared context loaded first, then local project docs (shared context default split shown in docs as ~30% but examples also show 40%).

Summary (what happens)
- Snipara uses the standard OAuth 2.0 Device Flow (RFC 8628) so users can authenticate an MCP client without copying API keys.
- User experience: run a CLI command, the CLI shows a URL and a short code; the user opens that URL in a browser, enters the code and completes login/consent. The CLI then obtains tokens from the server.

How the protocol works in Snipara (documented facts)
1. Device polling
   - The client polls the token endpoint POST /api/oauth/device/token to exchange the device_code for an access token.
   - Example request body:
     {
       "grant_type": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code",
       "device_code": "abc123...",
       "client_id": "snipara_cli"
     }
   - While the user has not yet completed authentication the token endpoint returns a pending response:
     { "error": "authorization_pending" }
   - On success the endpoint returns an access token (and refresh token):
     {
       "access_token": "snipara_at_...",
       "token_type": "Bearer",
       "expires_in": 2592000,
       "refresh_token": "snipara_rt_..."
     }

2. Token format and validation
   - OAuth access tokens use the prefix snipara_at_.
   - Tokens are validated via a DB lookup and expiry check (validate_oauth_token()) and scopes are enforced.

3. How the MCP client chooses credentials
   - The MCP client first checks for a stored OAuth token at ~/.snipara/tokens.json.
   - If no valid OAuth token is found it falls back to API key authentication (X-API-Key header).
   - OAuth requests use the Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_... header.

Troubleshooting / alternatives (documented)
- If OAuth should be skipped and the client forced to use an API key, you can:
  - Set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true in the client environment to skip reading ~/.snipara/tokens.json and use the API key.
  - Or remove/rename ~/.snipara/tokens.json (e.g., mv ~/.snipara/tokens.json ~/.snipara/tokens.json.bak).
- If an OAuth token is invalid or expired the server returns HTTP 401 with guidance to re-authenticate (e.g., re-authenticate at https://snipara.com/dashboard or run the quickstart).

References in the docs
- OAuth Device Flow description (RFC 8628): users run CLI → visit URL → enter code → authenticate in browser.
- POST /api/oauth/device/token (request/response examples).
- Token format and validate_oauth_token() details.
- ~/.snipara/tokens.json, SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH, and fallback to API key.

If you want, I can extract the exact example responses/requests or show the minimal client polling loop using only the documented request/response examples.

Snipara uses a strict layered architecture with a one-way import hierarchy:

- Routes (apps/web/src/app/api/) → Services (lib/services/) → Repositories (lib/db/queries/) → Utils & Types (no dependencies)

Key rules from the docs:
- Lower layers cannot import from higher layers (one-way dependency flow).
- All DB access must go through the repository layer (lib/db/queries/) — never call Prisma directly in route handlers.
- Route handlers are thin: authenticate, validate (Zod), delegate to a service or repository, and return a response.
- Services handle external APIs (lazy init + retry examples given); utils & types have no dependencies.

These points are all stated in the provided documentation.

Short answer (from the docs)
- Snipara will reduce a 500K‑token codebase to a much smaller, focused context — the docs state “From 500K tokens to ~5K relevant tokens” (listed under “Solution: Context Optimization as a Service”) and describe a context reduction figure (“90% Context Reduction”).
- 5K tokens easily fit inside a 128K token LLM context window, so the LLM can consume the optimized context without needing the full 500K corpus.

How Snipara achieves this (documented)
- Token budgeting: client specifies max_tokens and Snipara selects sections to fit that budget (rlm_context_query).
- Greedy selection by relevance: candidate sections are ranked and highest‑relevance sections are added until the token budget is reached.
- Smart truncation: if only part of a section fits, Snipara truncates at sentence boundaries and marks the section as truncated.
- Session context persistence and suggestions: session context can be included and Snipara returns suggested related docs.
- Implementation is LLM‑agnostic and Snipara does not run inference (clients use their own LLM).

What the docs say about cost savings
- The docs include a “Token Efficiency” concept and metrics (compression_ratio, token_reduction_pct, cost_savings_pct) for measuring savings, and they explicitly list the large context reduction (500K → ~5K).
- The docs do NOT provide concrete dollar amounts or example billing calculations for a specific LLM or pricing plan.

Short answer
- Use rlm_context_query for normal documentation Q&A, code lookups, and any single-step query with a clear answer.
- Use rlm_decompose when the question is complex and you want to break it into sub-questions you can inspect, modify, and run (often followed by rlm_multi_query).

Why (from the docs)
- rlm_context_query is the primary full‑featured semantic search tool for documentation queries — best for single-context fetches like "How does auth work?", "Where is X implemented?", or "List API endpoints." It’s faster/cheaper and avoids extra orchestration.
  - Example request shown in docs: { "query":"authentication", "max_tokens":4000, "search_mode":"hybrid", ... }.
- rlm_decompose is a Power‑User tool meant to break complex problems into sub‑queries so you can inspect/modify the plan before executing. The docs show using rlm_decompose for complex tasks (e.g., "Implement user authentication with email verification") which returns sub-queries like ["login flow","JWT handling","email service","verification flow"] and is typically followed by running those sub-queries (e.g., via rlm_multi_query).

Concrete rule of thumb (documented)
- If it’s a straightforward doc question → start with rlm_context_query.
- If the task is multi-part/complex and you want to control or parallelize the work → use rlm_decompose to produce sub-queries, then run them (rlm_multi_query or individual rlm_context_query calls).

(These recommendations are taken directly from the provided Snipara documentation.)

According to the documentation, use these search modes:

- Keyword  
  - What it is: basic term / exact-match search.  
  - When to use: looking for a specific function, API endpoint discovery, error-code lookup, or other focused/precise queries.

- Semantic  
  - What it is: embedding-similarity search.  
  - When to use: high-level “How does X work?” questions, architecture understanding, or when you need concept-level matches rather than exact terms.

- Hybrid  
  - What it is: combined keyword + semantic (best of both).  
  - When to use: general documentation searches and best-practices queries where you want both precise hits and conceptually relevant results.

Notes from the docs:
- Semantic and hybrid searches are Pro+ features; on Free plans such requests are automatically downgraded to keyword search and the response will indicate search_mode_downgraded: true.

Short answer — available auth options (where they apply) and the security-relevant differences shown in the docs:

1) API Key authentication
- Where used: MCP endpoints and Inject API. Header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
  - MCP: accepts X-API-Key or Authorization: Bearer
  - Inject API: only accepts X-API-Key
- Format & storage: keys have the rlm_ prefix; server stores a SHA‑256 hash of the key in the DB (auth.py:29–95).
- Lookup behavior: the server hashes the provided key and looks up the hash. Project key is checked first, then a team key fallback if no project key is found.
- Key management: team keys are managed by team owners (create/list/revoke endpoints) and a team key works across all projects in the team.
- Visibility: UI/logs only show the first 8 chars when displaying a key.
- Confirmed security-relevant facts: stored as hashes (SHA‑256) in DB; there is project vs team scope (team keys are broader).

2) OAuth (device-flow) token authentication
- Where used: MCP endpoints (Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...)
- Device flow: standard RFC 8628 device-flow endpoints exist (example POST /api/oauth/device/token with grant_type urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code). Device flow returns access_token (snipara_at_...), refresh_token (snipara_rt_...), expires_in, etc.
- Token model & storage: tokens are stored in DB as hashed values (OAuthToken type: accessTokenHash, refreshTokenHash, tokenPrefix, scope, clientName, accessExpiresAt, refreshExpiresAt).
- Validation: tokens are validated by DB lookup + expiry check; scopes are enforced.
- Revocation & listing: GET /api/settings/tokens lists tokens; DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[tokenId] revokes a token (returns success true).
- Expiry/refresh: access tokens include expires_in and a refresh token may be issued; invalid/expired OAuth tokens produce a 401 with the documented message directing re-authentication.
- Confirmed security-relevant facts: tokens have explicit expirations, are stored/validated as hashed values, include scopes, and can be revoked.

3) Local/client caching and behavior affecting auth selection
- The client/desktop stores OAuth tokens in ~/.snipara/tokens.json. The MCP server/client will prefer stored OAuth tokens (tokens.json) and fall back to API key from environment if no token is present.
- Because tokens can be cached locally (tokens.json) and the MCP process may cache credentials, removing that file (or moving it) and restarting Claude Code forces use of the API key. Restarting the client/server may be required to clear cached credentials.

Security-differences summary (facts from the docs)
- Lifetime & revocation:
  - OAuth tokens: have expirations and refresh tokens; can be revoked via token API.
  - API keys: no documented short-lived expiry in the snippets shown; management (create/revoke) exists for team keys.
- Scope control:
  - OAuth tokens expose and enforce scopes (e.g., "mcp:read mcp:write") in the token model.
  - API keys are scoped by project vs team (project key vs team-wide key).
- Storage & validation:
  - Both API keys and OAuth tokens are stored/validated via hashed values in DB (API keys: SHA‑256 hashed; OAuth tokens stored as hashes).
- Transport & usage:
  - OAuth tokens are sent in Authorization: Bearer header.
  - API keys are sent in X-API-Key header (and accepted by MCP; Inject API requires X-API-Key).
- Local caching risk noted in docs:
  - OAuth tokens can be cached in ~/.snipara/tokens.json and server processes may cache credentials — docs provide a removal/restart procedure to force API-key auth.

Summary — how token budgets are allocated with shared context collections (facts from the docs)

- Category-level budgets (shared collections)
  - The shared-context docs describe a category budget split commonly quoted as:
    - MANDATORY: 40%
    - BEST_PRACTICES: 30%
    - GUIDELINES: 20%
    - REFERENCE: 10%
  - Example: rlm_shared_context(...) sample output shows a budget_allocation mapping (e.g. MANDATORY: 1139, BEST_PRACTICES: 854, ...), computed as percentages of the requested max_tokens.

- Project vs shared split (rlm_context_query / MCP Query Order)
  - MCP Query Order states shared context is loaded first and project docs are queried with the remaining budget.
  - rlm_context_query supports parameters that control this split (examples in docs):
    - include_shared_context: true
    - shared_context_budget_percent: e.g. 30 (meaning 30% of the token budget goes to shared context, 70% to project)
  - Other examples in the docs show different splits used in examples (e.g. “60% project + 40% shared” or default 30% shared), indicating the shared/project percentage is configurable and appears in example usages.

- Backend allocation logic (actual code behavior)
  - The repository contains an allocateTokenBudget implementation (apps/web/lib/services/token-budget.ts) that uses a DEFAULT_ALLOCATION by default:
    - project: 0.40
    - MANDATORY: 0.25
    - BEST_PRACTICES: 0.20
    - GUIDELINES: 0.10
    - REFERENCE: 0.05
  - allocateTokenBudget:
    - Groups docs into project docs and shared docs by category.
    - Allocates the project portion first (projectBudget = floor(totalBudget * allocation.project).
    - Allocates each shared category next using floor(totalBudget * allocation[category]).
    - Within each group it calls allocateWithinBudget.
  - allocateWithinBudget:
    - Sorts docs by relevanceScore (if present), then iterates adding documents until the category budget is exhausted.
    - Documents added while tokensUsed + doc.tokenCount <= budget are marked allocated: true with an allocationReason (e.g., "project allocation" or "MANDATORY allocation").
    - Documents that don’t fit are marked allocated: false with allocationReason 'exceeded budget'.

- Hard limits and collection truncation
  - The code defines limits (LIMITS object):
    - MAX_COLLECTIONS_PER_PROJECT: 10
    - MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION: 100_000
    - MAX_DOCS_PER_COLLECTION: 50
    - MIN_RELEVANCE_THRESHOLD: 0.2
  - enforceCollectionLimits will truncate a collection to fit MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION by keeping documents until the token cap is reached and dropping the rest.

- Practical takeaway
  - Shared context is included first and receives an explicit portion of the token budget (the shared/project split is configurable in queries).
  - Within shared context, budgets are split by category; which exact percentages apply depends on which layer you look at:
    - user-facing documentation/examples often show the 40/30/20/10 split for MANDATORY/BEST_PRACTICES/GUIDELINES/REFERENCE,
    - the backend allocateTokenBudget default in code uses a different allocation (project 40%, MANDATORY 25%, BEST_PRACTICES 20%, GUIDELINES 10%, REFERENCE 5%).
  - The backend algorithm prioritizes project docs (per its DEFAULT_ALLOCATION), then fills shared categories in priority order, selecting documents by relevance until each allocated sub-budget is used.

Short answer: because it’s the most secure, user-friendly, and standards-compliant way to let CLI users authenticate without copying/storing long-lived API keys or embedding client secrets.

Key reasons we chose OAuth Device Flow (RFC 8628)

- User experience / usability
  - Simple CLI flow: user runs a command, visits a URL, enters a short code in their browser — no manual copying long secrets.
  - Works across platforms and environments (terminals, remote machines, headless servers) where a browser may be separate from the CLI.

- Security
  - Avoids embedding or transmitting long-lived API keys in the terminal or scripts.
  - Access tokens are short‑lived and can be revoked or rotated; refresh tokens manage session continuity.
  - Limits exposure of credentials to the browser’s auth session rather than pasting secrets into potentially insecure terminals.

- Standards and interoperability
  - Device Flow is a well-known OAuth2 extension (RFC 8628) implemented by many providers — predictable behavior, libraries, and security reviews.
  - No need for redirect URIs or client secrets, which simplifies CLI client implementation.

- Fine-grained control
  - Token scopes let the server enforce read/write permissions.
  - Server-side validation (DB + expiry checks) and the token format (snipara_at_...) give us predictable handling and logging.

- Developer / ops benefits
  - Reduces support overhead: fewer incidents caused by leaked API keys or misconfigured credentials.
  - Easier to audit and revoke access centrally from the dashboard.
  - Works smoothly with our existing token endpoints (e.g., POST /api/oauth/device/token polling with authorization_pending / success responses) and server validation logic.

- Practical fit for MCP
  - Matches the goal of avoiding manual API key copying (explicitly called out in our docs).
  - Integrates with our CLI client and server token handling (access_token + refresh_token + expires_in) and falls back to API key auth when needed.

Implementation notes (how it behaves in our system)
- CLI performs the device flow, then polls POST /api/oauth/device/token with grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code.
- Pending responses return error: "authorization_pending"; success returns access_token (snipara_at_...), token_type, expires_in, and refresh_token.
- Server validates tokens via DB lookup + expiry checks and enforces scopes.
